paper_title: GRASP: Learning to Ground Social Reasoning in Multi-Person Non-Verbal Interactions

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Understanding social interactions requires reasoning over subtle non-verbal cues, yet current multimodal large language models (MLLMs) often fail to identify who interacts with whom in multi-person videos. We introduce GRASP, a large-scale social reasoning dataset that connects high-level social QA with fine-grained gaze and deictic gesture events. GRASP contains 290K question--answer pairs over 46K videos totaling 749 hours, organized by a 16-category taxonomy spanning gaze, gesture, and joint gaze--gesture reasoning, together with GRASP-Bench for evaluation. Unlike prior resources that focus on either isolated cues or high-level social QA, GRASP builds questions from identity-consistent gaze trajectories, deictic gestures, and their joint compositions into social events. Moreover, we propose Social Grounding Reward (SGR), a learning signal that uses these social events to encourage models to reason about the participants involved in each interaction. Experiments show that SGR improves performance on GRASP-Bench while maintaining zero-shot performance on related social video QA benchmarks.

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Understanding social interactions requires reasoning over subtle non-verbal cues, yet current multimodal large language models (MLLMs) often fail to identify who interacts with whom in multi-person videos. We introduce GRASP, large-scale social reasoning dataset that connects high-level social QA with fine-grained gaze and deictic gesture events. GRASP contains 290K questionanswer pairs over 46K videos totaling 749 hours, organized by 16-category taxonomy spanning gaze, gesture, and joint gazegesture reasoning, together with GRASP-Bench for evaluation. Unlike prior resources that focus on either isolated cues or high-level social QA, GRASP builds questions from identity-consistent gaze trajectories, deictic gestures, and their joint compositions into social events. Moreover, we propose Social Grounding Reward (SGR), learning signal that uses these social events to encourage models to reason about the participants involved in each interaction. Experiments show that SGR improves performance on GRASP-Bench while maintaining zero-shot performance on related social video QA benchmarks.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: In everyday social interactions, the meaning of conversation is often shaped and influenced in subtle but important ways by non-verbal cues such as gaze and gesture [28, 23, 41]. brief glance toward social partner, pointing at an object of interest, or moment of mutual eye contact can signal who is being addressed, what is being referenced, and how participants relate to one another. Interpreting such signals [40, 7, 82] is inherently challenging, as it requires reasoning about complex interactions between individuals from ambiguous visual cues. Humans naturally resolve this complexity by integrating multimodal information [20, 35] to infer social meaning and dynamics [17, 45]. This capacity for social understanding underpins collaborative human behavior [77] and it is key capability that can enable AI systems are to interact effectively with people, from embodied agents [84] to conversational systems [71]. Prior research has made significant progress toward perceiving individual social cues as low-level signals, with focus on gaze and gesture modalities. Gaze target estimation methods [13, 68, 69] can predict where person is looking and gesture recognition models [7, 34, 50] can classify hand movements into predefined categories. These works operate at the level of perceptual primitives, answering what cue is present. In contrast, this work is the first systematic attempt to address the question of how gaze and gesture should be interpreted in social context, where meaning arises from the context in which behavior is produced. Corresponding authors. Preprint. Figure 1: Example from GRASP. Multi-person social reasoning requires grounding subtle non-verbal cues in the correct participants over time. Existing MLLMs [19, 78] often take spurious scene-level shortcuts, whereas ours leverage evidence-aware supervision to reason from the relevant social event. The key hypothesis underlying this work is that modern MLLMs [52, 14, 51], which integrate visual perception with strong reasoning capabilities, can be utilized to integrate nonverbal cues and infer their meaning in the context of social understanding. Several works have introduced new social understanding tasks such as multi-party interaction reasoning [33, 37], speaker target identification [40, 45, 64], and social video QA [56, 62]. However, current MLLMs have only limited ability to capture social interactions as grounded visual events: (i) while they can recognize people and actions in the visual scene, they often fail to organize them into temporally grounded social events among participants, making it difficult to identify the relevant cue, participants, and timing of an interaction, e.g., who is looking at, gesturing toward, or responding to whom. (ii) Their reasoning often does not attend to the fine-grained visual evidence that determines these relations, relying instead on linguistic or scene-level priors as shown in Fig. 1. Effective social interaction understanding requires grounding reasoning in non-verbal cues such as gaze direction, deictic gestures, and their joint coordination across participants, but large-scale supervision that jointly connects these fine-grained cues with high-level social reasoning remains scarce. In this paper, we close this gap by introducing Grounded Reasoning And Social Perception (GRASP), the first large-scale dataset for grounded social understanding that jointly supports both high-level social reasoning and fine-grained gazegesture grounding in multi-person videos. Existing social datasets provide fragmented supervision, focusing on either single fine-grained cue such as gaze [68, 13, 30, 67] or gesture [7], or high-level social QA tasks [88], rather than jointly supervising how multiple non-verbal cues ground social reasoning. Building such concurrent supervision manually is prohibitively expensive, requiring temporally precise, identity-consistent annotations of multi-person gazegesture dynamics. For scalability, we develop bottom-up pipeline that extracts structured social interactions from video by detecting gaze and deictic gestures across individuals, organizing them into social events (e.g., mutual gaze, joint attention, gaze following), and modeling their joint compositions where gaze and gesture together convey meaning. GRASP contains 290K QA instances organized under 16-category taxonomy, grounded in 245K interaction events across 46K video clips totaling 749 hours. In addition, we provide GRASP-Bench, curated benchmark of 1K test items. An additional key question that we address in this work is how such structured social events can provide effective learning signals for MLLMs. Existing RL-based policy optimization approaches (e.g., GRPO [70, 53]) encourage models to generate reasoning before answering, but they do not explicitly tie this reasoning to the relevant social cues. In social scenarios, as exemplified in Fig. 1, this can lead models either to produce spurious reasoning traces, consistent with overthinking behavior [73, 85, 11], or to arrive at answers through linguistic and scene-level shortcuts. Accordingly, we introduce Social Grounding Reward, learning signal that verifies whether the models reasoning references the correct participants involved in the underlying social events. Rather than relying on complex reward functions, we provide direct signal Did the model attend to the right person? that shifts supervision toward evidence-aware reasoning. To demonstrate the utility of GRASP, we post-train MLLMs with our data and policy, achieving competitive performance on GRASP-Bench. Beyond in-domain evaluation, our model also generalizes to three social reasoning benchmarks [40, 45, 43], indicating that social grounding learned from GRASP transfers across task formulations. Collectively, these findings demonstrate that large-scale 2 participant-level social data enables MLLMs to move beyond primitive perception and reason about social interactions from non-verbal cues. Our contribution is three-fold: We construct GRASP and GRASP-Bench for training and evaluating MLLMs on grounded social reasoning over multi-person gaze and gesture interactions, using bottom-up pipeline that scales participant-level social event annotation. We propose Social Grounding Reward, simple yet effective RL signal that directly verifies whether models reasoning attends to the correct participants involved in the underlying social events, teaching MLLMs to ground social reasoning in the relevant non-verbal evidence. We demonstrate that our framework achieves strong performance on GRASP-Bench, generalizes to other social reasoning benchmarks, and provides detailed analyses of where and why social grounding is most beneficial.

sections.title: 2 Related Works
sections.content: Gaze and Gesture in Human Social Behavior. Non-verbal cues, particularly gaze and gesture, are fundamental to human social interaction, often indicating intention and the targets of utterances [41, 47]. Interpreting these behaviors has driven significant research in computer vision across two parallel tracks. In gaze understanding, several works have addressed the task of gaze target estimation, leveraging benchmarks such as GazeFollow [68] and VideoAttentionTarget [13]. Approaches have evolved from fusion-based methods that process depth, pose, and head crops separately [68, 31, 18, 5, 26], to decoding methods leveraging visual foundation model representations [72, 69]. related task area is identifying social gaze dynamics such as mutual gaze and joint attention [54, 16, 17, 25]. While some recent methods for gaze target estimation aim to integrate VLM priors into gaze pipelines [27, 55, 65], specialized models for gaze target estimation remain the dominant approach. In contrast to these gaze-specific models, our work demonstrates how to equip MLLMs with the ability to not only estimate gaze, but jointly leverage it as cue for higher level social understanding. Concurrently, gesture recognition has been extensively explored through diverse datasets encompassing RGB-D videos (LD-ConGR [50]), high-resolution images (HaGRID [34]), egocentric views (EgoGesture [90]), and multi-person social contexts (SocialGesture [7]). Modeling efforts in this domain have similarly advanced toward leveraging foundation models, including MLLMs and VLMs [48, 6]. Despite extensive progress in these individual domains, critical gap remains in jointly modeling gaze and gesture to infer social intention [8], an integration essential for holistic understanding of social behavior. Multi-modal Large Language Models in Social AI. Recent advances in MLLMs have demonstrated remarkable video understanding across diverse domains, including multimedia analysis, and human action recognition [36, 89, 19, 87, 91]. Leading proprietary models, such as the Gemini 3 [24] and GPT-5 [63] series, alongside open-source alternatives like Qwen-VL series [3], leverage extensive context windows to process complex video inputs. While these models have set new records on challenging video question-answering benchmarks [21, 22, 29], critical limitation remains: many rely disproportionately on their base language models. This reliance often leads to commonsense biases in social contexts, where models may provide accurate answers based on linguistic patterns even without accessing the visual data [44, 41]. While recent datasets [88, 39, 40, 56, 37] have attempted to address this, most existing benchmarks focus on broad social reasoning, overlooking fine-grained interpersonal cues such as gaze shifts and subtle social gestures [45, 46, 74, 86, 76]. In this paper, we bridge this gap by introducing novel benchmark and novel post-training strategy designed to enhance the fine-grained social intelligence of MLLMs.

sections.title: 3 GRASP Dataset: Grounded Reasoning And Social Perception
sections.content: To enable social reasoning grounded in non-verbal cues, we first curate video sources spanning broad range of multi-person social interaction types, including multi-party TV dialogue [43, 81], social intelligence reasoning [88], multi-speaker conversation [60], and embodied multi-person behavior [57]. We further include social deduction gameplay [39, 7], which we augment with 3 Figure 2: Overview of the GRASP construction pipeline and QA examples. We convert multi-person videos into person-consistent gaze and gesture events, compose them into structured social QA pairs, and apply subset validation with human feedback for quality control. additional online video sources, as such contents contain dense multi-person interactions with rich social signals. Our dataset comprises 46K videos, from which we construct structured representations of social interactions by extracting person-consistent gaze trajectories and deictic gestures and integrating them into higher-order social events. All extracted events are anchored to track person identities, providing structured supervision for both QA generation and our grounding reward. We illustrate the overall collection process in Fig. 2, and provide details in Appendix A. 3.1 Data Collection Pipeline Multi-person Identification & Gaze Annotation. Given raw video , we first split it into shorter segments {Vs}S s=1 at scene cuts using PySceneDetect to preserve coherent social interactions. Within each segment, our goal is to obtain person-consistent gaze trajectories in multi-party scenes, so that every gaze observation can be retained with stable identity across time. To do so, we design multi-stage process that jointly tracks person identities and aligns gaze observations across frames. person = {bt We leverage an off-the-shelf detection model, SAM 3 [10] with the text prompt people to obtain temporally consistent bounding boxes Bt i}iP , where denotes the set of tracked person IDs. While the model provides stable identity tracking, it cannot predict precise facial localization for the subsequent gaze estimation process. Accordingly, we detect face location Bt j=1 using RetinaFace [15], where Mt denotes the number of detected faces at time t, and associate each face with tracked identity using Hungarian matching [38] between face boxes and the person boxes. For each person at time t, we then use the matched face center ct as point prompt together with the video frame, and feed them to GazeAnywhere [9] to estimate normalized gaze point ) [0, 1]2. This yields person-consistent gaze trajectories {gt = (xt gt t=1. The resulting triplet {(i, bt i, gt t=1, where P, preserves person identity, spatial location, and gaze target over time, which are then used to define higher-order social events. face = {f i, yt )}T }Mt }T Gaze Feature Extraction & Event Detection. Raw per-frame gaze observations {gt } are inherently noisy due to missing detections and camera motion. To obtain robust temporal features, we first apply simple interpolation scheme within each segment to handle short gaps. We then compute relative gaze velocity by measuring gaze motion relative to the face center. For each person at time t, we define their gaze direction as dt i. To capture group-level attention dynamics, we compute convergence score that measures how closely gaze points cluster at each time step. Specifically, we compute the median distance between individual gaze points gt and gt), where st [0, 1]. their centroid, and map it to normalized score st = exp(α medianigt i, and estimate velocity as tdt = gt ct 4 From these features we define five social gaze events, grounded in established taxonomies of gaze behavior [59, 32]: mutual gaze (bidirectional gaze between two participants), joint attention (multiple participants attending to common target), gaze following (one participant shifting gaze to anothers prior target), attention capture (simultaneous gaze shifts across multiple participants), and sudden gaze shift (per-person saccade), resulting in 169K gaze events across the corpus (details in Appendix. A). Gesture Detection & Unified Social Graph. Robust detection of communicative hand gestures in untrimmed, multi-person video is challenging due to temporal ambiguity, identity confusion, and the lack of precise grounding signals. Following prior gesture taxonomies in social interaction [58, 7], we focus on deictic gestures and prompt the model to identify four types, namely pointing, showing, giving, and reaching. We leverage Gemini [24] as an annotator, augmented with structured visual cues. For each video, we render person-tracked bounding boxes Bt person together with their corresponding IDs and per-frame timestamps directly onto each frame. This provides explicit spatio-temporal grounding, allowing the model to directly read timestamp from the frame and casting temporal localization into an OCR-like problem, thereby improving the reliability of temporal grounding. We further employ self-refinement loop in which low-confidence predictions are iteratively regenerated until convergence, yielding 76K reliable gesture instances with person-level initiator and target identities. We then merge gaze events and gesture annotations into unified social graph for each video. Each node represents detected social event with structured metadata, including event type, participating identities, and temporal boundaries, along with modality-specific attributes such as gaze convergence or gesture targets. Joint-modal interactions are captured by linking gaze and gesture events that co-occur within temporal window, enabling reasoning over joint gazegesture patterns. The resulting graph contains 245K events across 46K videos and serves as the foundation for both our QA taxonomy and the grounding supervision used during training. 3.2 Social Reasoning QA Taxonomy Our dataset is designed to probe not only the perception of individual cues, but also the ability to reason about social dynamics arising from multi-person interactions. Accordingly, we carefully define 16-category QA taxonomy with increasing levels of difficulty (examples in Appendix. D.1). Gaze reasoning (T1T6) spans tasks from perception to inference, including gaze target identification (T1), event classification (T2), mutual gaze recognition (T3), gaze following (T4), temporal reasoning (T5), and group attention dynamics (T6). While T1T2 focus on identifying who looks where, T5T6 require reasoning over temporal ordering and multi-person attention dynamics. Gesture reasoning (G1G6) covers gesture recognition (G1), type classification (G2), temporal reasoning (G3), reciprocal patterns (G4), frequency (G5), and sequence chains (G6), with higherlevel categories requiring multi-step interaction reasoning. Gazegesture joint reasoning (J1J4) requires integrating gaze and gesture signals to reason over co-occurring non-verbal cues, including temporal alignment (J1), gaze response to gesture (J2), eye contact during interaction (J3), and cross-modal person dynamics (J4). Social Reasoning QA Generation. QA pairs are generated from structured event metadata derived from gaze and gesture interactions using closed-source model [24]. Each question is constructed by querying key attributes such as participant identities, temporal intervals, and interaction types, ensuring that answers are directly verifiable without exhaustive manual annotation. To provide sufficient temporal context, each QA is anchored around target event and augmented with short temporal window from its surrounding frames. We design this process to produce diverse questions spanning both perception and higher-order reasoning over multi-person interactions. To ensure data reliability, we further conduct human validation on subset of generated QA pairs, where sampled QA instances are checked for event consistency, answer verifiability, and ambigu5 Figure 3: GRASP taxonomy and statistics. ity. The assessment is used to refine the generation and filtering criteria, providing an additional quality-control loop before producing the final dataset of 290K QA pairs grounded in structured gaze and gesture events. We provide examples in Fig. 2 and statistics in Fig. 3 and Appendix. D.1. Please see additional details in Appendix B.

sections.title: 4 Learning to Ground Social Reasoning
sections.content: Preliminaries. GRPO [70] provides an effective framework for training MLLMs with reasoning capabilities [19, 12, 87]. Given video-question pair query q, the policy πθ generates reasoning trajectories {y1, . . . , yK}. The policy is optimized by maximizing the following objective: max πθ Eyπθold (cid:34) (cid:88) i=1 πθ(yi q) πθold(yi q) ˆAi β DKL(πθ πref) , (1) (cid:35) where πθold is the policy from the previous iteration, πref is fixed reference (e.g., SFT model), and β controls the KL regularization. The advantage for each trajectory is computed relative to the group: ˆAi = ri mean({r1, . . . , rK}) std({r1, . . . , rK}) , (2) where ri denotes the reward assigned to trajectory yi. In practice, rewards are typically defined using simple verifiable signals such as answer correctness and output format consistency. Each trajectory is formatted with special tokens, where the model generates intermediate reasoning within <think> and outputs the final answer within <answer>. 4.1 Training Strategy for Social Reasoning We adopt two-stage training strategy: (i) warm-up the model with structured SFT responses that explicitly separate gaze and gesture evidence and (ii) RL post-training with rewards for correctness, format consistency, structural grounding, and social grounding. Stage 1: Structured Format Supervised Fine-Tuning. Applying RL directly to the base models induces cold-start problem, as reasoning about fine-grained non-verbal cues is largely absent from existing multimodal corpora. Thus, stochastic rollouts rarely produce trajectories that reference relevant gaze or gesture events, resulting in reward landscape that is too sparse to effectively bootstrap learning. Following prior works [19, 66] that introduces warm-up before policy optimization, we first utilize the 52K GRASP subset for SFT, where each target response is formatted as: <think> <gaze> Egaze </gaze> <gesture> Eges </gesture> </think> <answer> </answer>, where Egaze, Eges enumerate the temporally grounded gaze, gesture events drawn from the social graph, and is an answer for those events. This warm-up teaches models to decompose social reasoning into its non-verbal components before arriving at an answer, yielding reference model πref that can generate the dual-tag format and provides stable starting point for the subsequent RL stage. Stage 2: Grounded Reinforcement Post-Training. Starting from πref, we continue training on the GRASP dataset with policy optimization. Given video-question pair and group of rollouts {yi}K i=1, we define the scalar reward for trajectory yi as weighted combination of four signals: ri = λacc racc + λfmt rfmt + λstr rstr = 1[ˆai = ai] enforces answer correctness, and rfmt + λgnd rgnd (3) = 1[yi F] ensures that the output where racc adheres to the required structure, where denotes the set of valid outputs. We further include structural term rstr that preserves the use of <gaze> and <gesture> tags, and social grounding term rgnd that encourages the model to identify the relevant participants in the interaction. , 4.2 Social Grounding Reward for Social Learning Signal To make the reasoning trace reflect non-verbal cues, we introduce structural grounding term that encourages the model to express its observations through the dual-tags. interaction. Unlike perception tasks that rely on categorical labels, social interactions are inherently relational, requiring the model to identify involved participants and their interactions. Let pred denote the set of person identities mentioned within the <gaze> and <gesture> blocks of trajectory yi, and let gt(q) denote the set of participants involved in the target social event specified in q. The grounding reward is defined using weighted precisionrecall formulation: = wi (P pred rgnd , gt(q)), wi = 1 + R(P pred , gt(q)), (5) where (), R() measure precision, recall over participant IDs by comparing predicted and target sets based on their overlap. By balancing coverage and selectivity, our reward penalizes both noisy mentions of all visible individuals and overly narrow predictions that miss key participants, which encourages models to identify relevant interacting individuals without introducing irrelevant ones.

sections.title: 5 Experiments
sections.content: Implementation Details. We build our framework on two baselines [3, 75]. The input videos are sampled at 2 fps and rendered with person-tracked bounding boxes, where each individual is marked with IDs (e.g., P0, P1, . . . ) to support grounded references. Training proceeds in two stages: we first fine-tune the models on the 52K small subset of GRASP, followed by our policy optimization on the 238K MCQ subset with = 8 rollouts with KL regularization. The composite reward is set as (λacc, λfmt, λstr, λgnd) = (1.0, 0.1, 0.05, 0.2). All models are trained in bfloat16 on 8NVIDIA H200 GPUs with DeepSpeed ZeRO-2, and full training details are provided in the Appendix C. Experimental Setting. We evaluate on GRASP-Bench, curated test set covering 16 categories, as our in-domain benchmark, and on three public social-reasoning benchmarks to assess crossbenchmark generalization: MMSI [40], Online-MMSI [45], and TVQA+ [43]. MMSI probes multiparty social reasoning through three tasks, including speaker target identification (STI), pronoun coreference resolution (PCR), and mentioned-player prediction (MPP), while Online-MMSI evaluates the same tasks under causally truncated setting that admits only past frames. TVQA+ measures spatio-temporally grounded video QA over multi-character dialogues. We compare against three groups of MLLMs: (i) proprietary models [2, 63, 24], (ii) instruction-tuned models [4, 80, 1, 3, 75], and (iii) RL-based reasoning models [78, 87, 19, 12, 80, 1, 3, 75]. 5.1 Main Results on GRASP-Bench We report the main results on GRASP-Bench in Tab. 1. Overall, our socially-grounded RL consistently outperforms both supervised fine-tuned models and existing RL post-training baselines across all reasoning types. The gains are particularly evident in gaze and gesture reasoning, where accurate identification of interacting individuals is critical, as well as in cross-modal reasoning that requires 7 Table 2: Cross-benchmark generalization results on MMSI, Online-MMSI, and TVQA+. These results indicate that explicitly grounding reasoning in nonverbal evidence leads to more reliable and coherent social understanding, rather than relying on coarse visual perception or language priors. notable trend is that SFT models are generally more competitive than RL baselines, suggesting that generic RL post-training for longer reasoning does not necessarily improve social understanding. Without an explicit grounding signal, additional reasoning can amplify spurious assumptions and distract models from the non-verbal cues and participant relations that determine the answer. In contrast, SGR rewards reasoning that refers to the correct participants in the underlying event, retaining useful deliberation while suppressing unsupported shortcuts. We provide qualitative comparisons in Appendix D, where RL baselines often produce plausible but ungrounded rationales, whereas our method reasons from the relevant people and non-verbal cues. 5.2 Generalization to Other Social Understanding Datasets We evaluate cross-benchmark generalization on other social understanding datasets [40, 45, 43] in zero-shot setting, without further task-specific fine-tuning. As shown in Tab. 2, SGR-trained models outperform or remain competitive with corresponding instruction-tuned backbones and RL post-training baselines. Given that our models post-training is performed only on GRASP, these results indicate that (i) the models do not over-specialize to the GRASP-Bench format and (ii) SGR training is beneficial for broader social reasoning tasks beyond GRASPs gaze and gesture focused questions. Furthermore, we provide qualitative reasoning-trace comparisons for these benchmarks in Appendix D.2, demonstrating that SGR-trained models effectively leverage gaze and gesture as intermediate cues on these benchmarks. 5.3 Ablation and Diagnostic Analysis Detailed Comparison with GRPO. We conduct detailed comparison with GRPO [70] to understand whether improvements arise from stronger optimization alone or from explicitly grounding social reasoning. While the RL method encourages models to generate reasoning before answering, it does not enforce that such reasoning is aligned with the correct social evidence. As result, models 8 Figure 4: Per-category comparison on GRASP-Bench across baselines including SFT, reasoning, GRPO-tuned, and ours. Figure 5: Grounded participant precisionaccuracy on GRASP-Bench across various reasoning baselines. Marker size reflects the average number of novel participants mentioned in the reasoning trace. often produce verbose but ungrounded reasoning traces that fail to identify the relevant individuals involved in social interactions. As shown in Tab. 3, incorporating GRPO on top of the baseline yields moderate improvements, indicating that reasoning supervision alone provides some benefit. However, adding our SGR leads to substantially larger gains across all metrics, highlighting the importance of explicitly grounding reasoning in non-verbal cues. Fig. 4 further provides detailed breakdown across all categories. While instruction model and zero-shot reasoning models exhibit inconsistent performance, and GRPO improves certain categories, it still struggles to reliably capture fine-grained social cues. In contrast, ours consistently improves performance, demonstrating that our grounding reasoning leads to coherent social understanding. Scaling with Model Size. We analyze model-size scaling with SGR in Tab. 4 and compare the corresponding grounding behavior in Fig. 5. Across the baseline backbones [3, 75], average accuracy improves with model size, increasing from 46.2 to 50.4 and from 45.3 to 52.6, respectively. In Fig. 5, the scaled SGR-trained models remain in the high grounded-participant-precision region while achieving increasingly strong GRASP-Bench accuracy. This shows that the scaling gains come together with participant-grounded reasoning, rather than only stronger answer prediction. Grounding Precision Analysis. To verify whether our reward indeed encourages models to identify the correct participants, we analyze grounding precision across reasoning models. We measure the proportion of correctly mentioned participants in the reasoning trace, alongside overall accuracy. As shown in Fig. 5, the precision across baselines exhibits positive correlation with accuracy (ρ = 0.64), indicating that correctly identifying relevant individuals is closely tied to successful social reasoning. We further observe distinct differences in grounding behavior across models. Many RL baselines achieve moderate accuracy despite low grounding precision, often mentioning multiple irrelevant participants, which leads to noisy reasoning traces and higher false positives. In contrast, our method achieves higher grounding precision with fewer participant mentions, reflecting more selective and targeted reasoning. This behavior aligns with the objective of SGR, which explicitly encourages the model to focus on the correct interacting individuals rather than enumerating all visible candidates.

sections.title: 6 Discussion and Conclusion
sections.content: Discussion. We introduced GRASP as large-scale social reasoning dataset grounded in finegrained non-verbal events. Constructing such supervision is inherently challenging: despite our quality-control pipeline and human validation (Appendix A.7), the resulting annotations can still be noisy in ambiguous scenes. Moreover, we propose SGR, simple but effective reward signal that encourages models to ground reasoning in the participants involved in the target interaction. We also examine possible ID-copying shortcut through ID corruption analysis (Appendix C.3); the observed degradation indicates that copying question IDs alone does not explain the learned behavior. Still, 9 SGR mainly verifies participant-level grounding, and incorporating stricter temporal alignment and richer social cues remains important future work. Broader limitations are discussed in Appendix E. Conclusion. We introduced GRASP, large-scale dataset for grounded social reasoning that connects high-level social QA with fine-grained gaze and gesture events in multi-person videos. GRASP provides 290K QA pairs over 46K videos, organized by 16-category taxonomy, together with GRASP-Bench for social reasoning evaluation. Moreover, we proposed SGR, reward signal that uses structured social events to encourage models to reason about the participants involved in each interaction. Experiments show that participant-grounded supervision improves performance on GRASP-Bench while preserving zero-shot performance on related social video QA tasks.

sections.title: A Detailed GRASP Construction
sections.content: A.1 Video Pre-processing and Person-Consistent Gaze Trajectories To construct temporally grounded social interactions, we first normalize raw videos into short interaction-centered clips. Long-form videos are segmented using scene-cut detection (PySceneDetect2). We enforce minimum segment length of 45 seconds by merging overly short adjacent segments, and maximum segment length of 120 seconds by splitting long segments at the nearest available scene boundary. All downstream gaze processing samples videos at 2 fps, so the temporal resolution of gaze-derived annotations is 0.5 seconds. For each processed clip, we estimate person-consistent gaze trajectories through multi-stage identityaware pipeline. We first detect and track people using SAM 3 [10] across frames using text-prompted person tracking model with the prompt people, yielding temporally consistent person bounding boxes and participant identities. Since these person tracks do not provide sufficiently precise facial localization for gaze estimation, we additionally detect faces using [15] in each sampled frame. Each detected face is associated with tracked person identity by solving Hungarian matching problem [38] between face boxes and the upper half of the corresponding person boxes, which approximates the head region. Matches with zero overlap are rejected to avoid assigning face to an unrelated body track. After face-person association, we estimate the gaze target with AnyGaze [9] for each matched face. The gaze estimator predicts normalized gaze point gt ) [0, 1]2 for person at time t, together with an in-frame indicator. The final per-frame representation therefore aligns each participant identity with person box, face box, gaze point, and gaze validity flag. This identitypreserving representation is the basis for all subsequent gaze feature computation and social event detection. = (xt i, yt Table 5: Key components used to construct person-consistent gaze trajectories. Component Scene segmentation Frame sampling Person tracking Face detection Role Split long-form videos into coherent interaction segments. Normalize the temporal resolution for downstream gaze processing. Maintain stable participant across frames. Localize faces for gaze estimation. identities Face-person matching Assign each detected face to tracked Key setting 45120s segments 2 fps; 0.5s interval Text prompt people; confidence threshold 0.45 Per sampled frame; confidence threshold 0.54 Hungarian matching on the upper 50% person region; zero-overlap matches rejected [0, 1]2 Gaze estimation participant identity. Predict where each participant is looking. Normalized frame coordinate gt with in-frame flag A.2 Gaze Feature Extraction Raw gaze trajectories contain missing observations caused by missed face detections, out-of-frame gaze, occlusion, and abrupt camera changes. We therefore apply conservative interpolation scheme before computing temporal features. Missing gaps of up to 3 frames are linearly interpolated, as shown in Tab. 6 . Gaps of 410 frames are filled by carrying forward the last valid observation with decayed confidence. Longer gaps are left missing. To prevent spurious continuity across cuts or large viewpoint changes, interpolation is blocked whenever the temporal gap exceeds 3 seconds or the face displacement exceeds 30% of the frame width. To reduce sensitivity to camera motion, gaze velocity is computed relative to the persons face center rather than from raw gaze point displacement. Let ct denote the face center of person at time t. We define the face-centered gaze direction as and estimate gaze velocity as: = gt dt ct i, vt = dt dt1 . 2We use AdaptiveDetector with default setup in https://github.com/Breakthrough/PySceneDetect. 15 This formulation captures changes in where person looks relative to their own head position, making it more robust to camera pans and global frame motion. We also compute group-level convergence score to identify shared attention. For all valid in-frame gaze points at time t, we compute their centroid gt and measure the median distance from individual gaze points to this centroid. The convergence score is st = exp (cid:0)3.0 medianigt gt(cid:1) . Using the median distance makes the score robust to outliers, such as one participant looking away while several others attend to common region. Table 6: Missing-gaze handling before temporal feature computation. Missing gap Strategy 3 frames 410 frames Carry forward > 10 frames Keep missing Linear interpolation Assigned confidence 1.0 0.1 gap 0.5 exp(0.2 gap) A.3 Social Gaze Event Detection From the per-person velocities and group convergence features, we detect five classes of social gaze events: sudden gaze shift, joint attention, gaze following, attention capture, and mutual gaze, as summarized in Tab. 7. Each event type is defined through an operational detector over identityconsistent gaze trajectories. Table 7: Operational definitions for the five social gaze event types used in GRASP. Event type Sudden gaze shift Joint attention Gaze following Attention capture Mutual gaze Event definition One participant rapidly changes gaze direction. At least two participants attend to common region. One participant looks toward another participants previous gaze target. At least three participants shift gaze within short temporal window. Two participants look at each others face regions. Main criterion vt > 0.7 st 0.6, duration 0.5s distance < 0.03, lag 1.02.0s vt > 0.4 for 3 persons bidirectional face-box hit, duration 1.0s For sudden gaze shifts, we cluster consecutive high-velocity frames using maximum temporal gap of 0.6 seconds and retain events whose durations fall between 0.5 and 1.5 seconds. Joint attention is detected by identifying frames with high convergence score and at least two visible faces, then clustering consecutive frames whose participant sets have at least 70% overlap. Participants far from the convergence center are filtered out to avoid including late joiners or peripheral observers. Gaze following is detected by comparing participants current gaze target against another participants prior high-confidence measured gaze target. We only use measured gaze histories for this detector and require temporal lag between 1.0 and 2.0 seconds. Attention capture is detected when at least three visible participants exhibit high gaze velocity within short temporal window, indicating simultaneous shift of attention. Mutual gaze is detected through bidirectional hit test: person As gaze must fall inside person Bs face box while person Bs gaze falls inside person As face box. To reduce false positives, mutual gaze uses measured gaze only, not interpolated gaze, and applies 2% bounding-box margin. Only high-confidence gaze events are passed to the unified graph construction stage. In the final construction pipeline, downstream processing keeps gaze events with confidence at least 0.9. A.4 Deictic Gesture Annotation In addition to gaze, GRASP models communicative hand gestures that direct attention or indicate interaction targets. We use Gemini 3.1 Pro [24] to detect four deictic gesture types: pointing, showing, giving, and reaching (Tab. 8). To provide explicit identity and temporal grounding, each input video is rendered with tracked person boxes, visible participant labels 0, 1, . . ., and timestamp overlays = X.XXs. The model is instructed to scan the entire video, identify all visible deictic gestures, assign initiator and target IDs from the overlay, and use the displayed timestamp for start and end times. The full gesture classification prompt is shown in Fig. 26. 16 We use strict and conservative gesture definition. gesture is annotated only when its type and intent are clearly identifiable from hand, finger, or arm motion alone. The prompt explicitly excludes inference from gaze direction, head pose, speech, facial expression, scene context, or gaze visualization artifacts. Ambiguous hand movements, beat gestures, casual arm motion, and resting poses are skipped. We require minimum duration of 1.0 second, allow simultaneous gestures by different participants, and return an empty set when no high-confidence deictic gesture is visible. We further apply an iterative verification-and-regeneration step. After the initial Gemini prediction, separate verification pass scores the predicted gesture set out of 7 points: 2 points for timeline alignment and 5 points for visual-context accuracy, including gesture type, initiator ID, target person ID, reliance on deictic hand/arm motion, and coverage of visible gesture events. Each subcriterion is scored as 1.0, 0.5, or 0.0. If the total score is at most 5/7, we regenerate the gesture prediction by providing the previous prediction and verification report as feedback. This process reduces timestamp errors, swapped participant identities, false positives from non-deictic motion, and missed salient gestures. Each retained gesture is stored with structured fields including gesture type, initiator identity, target type, target person identity when applicable, start time, end time, and confidence. The final construction keeps gestures with confidence at least 0.85. Free-form target descriptions and reasoning text produced by Gemini are used only as contextual language for graph and QA generation. Ground-truth answers are derived only from structured, verifiable fields such as gesture type, initiator identity, target identity, and temporal boundaries. Table 8: Gesture types annotated in GRASP and their conservative annotation criteria. Gesture type Pointing Showing Giving Reaching Annotation criterion Clear arm, hand, or finger extension toward specific person or target. Intentionally orienting or presenting an object for another participant to inspect. Offering an object to another participant with transfer intent. Directed hand extension toward an object or target with acquisition intent. A.5 Unified Social Graph Construction After extracting gaze events and gesture annotations, we merge them into unified social graph for each video. The graph represents social interactions as temporally ordered event nodes. Each node stores the event source, event type, involved participant identities, start and end times, confidence, and modality-specific attributes such as gaze convergence statistics, leader-follower identities, gesture initiators, or gesture targets. Before graph construction, gaze events and gestures are filtered using their respective confidence thresholds. Gaze events are retained at confidence 0.9, and gestures are retained at confidence 0.85. All timestamps are snapped to 0.5-second intervals to match the 2 fps processing resolution. Events whose temporal boundaries exceed the video duration are removed. Duplicate events are removed when they share the same type, the same participants, and an overlapping temporal window. To support joint reasoning over coordinated non-verbal cues, we identify gazegesture pairs whose temporal distance is at most 3.0 seconds. These pairs become the basis for Joint Reasoning questions in categories J1J4. During graph pruning, gazegesture pairs are protected before single-modality events are removed, because randomly dropping one side of pair would destroy the evidence needed for joint reasoning. When video contains many events, we cap the graph at 25 events, prioritizing protected gazegesture pairs, event-type diversity, and high-confidence events. Table 9: Key filtering and merging parameters for unified social graph construction. Purpose Retain high-precision gaze events. Retain high-confidence gesture annotations. Match the 2 fps processing resolution. Link temporally adjacent gaze and gesture events. Control graph size while preserving key social evidence. Parameter Gaze confidence threshold Gesture confidence threshold Timestamp snapping Joint-pair proximity Maximum events per video Value 0.9 0.85 0.5s 3.0s 25 17 Modality Subcategory T1: Gaze Target ID Example Q: At around 2.7 seconds, who is Person 0 looking at? A: Person 2 T2: Gaze Event Classification Q: What best describes the interaction between Person 0 and Person 1 around 2.0 seconds? T3: Mutual Gaze Recognition Q: How long do Person 1 and Person 2 maintain eye contact starting at 1.5 seconds? A: Looking at the same thing Gaze A: 3.0 seconds T4: Gaze Following Detection Q: Which pair of people makes eye contact between 2.5 and 4.5 seconds? A: Person 3 and Person 5 T5: Temporal Gaze Reasoning Q: In the gaze following event between 1.0s and 3.0s, who looks at the target first? A: Person 6 T6: Group Attention Dynamics Q: During the shared attention event between 2.0 and 3.0 seconds, which person is NOT part of the group? G1: Gesture Recognition G2: Gesture Type G3: Gesture Temporal Gesture G4: Reciprocal Patterns G5: Gesture Frequency G6: Sequence Chains J1: Gaze-Gesture Alignment A: Person 3 Q: Between 3.0 and 5.0 seconds, who is Person 3 pointing at? A: Person 0 Q: What type of gesture does Person 1 perform at 3.0 seconds? A: Reaching Q: Between 2.5 and 5.0 seconds, Person 10 performs two gestures. Which happens first? A: Showing Q: What is the most common gesture type performed throughout the video clip? A: Pointing Q: Person 2 gives to Person 1 between 1.56.5s. Who does Person 1 give to next? A: Person 0 Q: At 2.5s, Person 3 reaches toward Person 2. Who is Person 2 simultaneously reaching toward? A: Person 1 Q: Which happens first for Person 0: their sudden gaze shift, or their pointing gesture towards Person 2? A: The sudden gaze shift happens first J2: Gaze Response to Gesture Q: Just as Person 0 starts pointing at card at 2.0s, who are they making eye contact with? Joint J3: Eye Contact / Interaction J4: Cross-Modal Dynamics A: Person 1 Q: Right before Person 1 starts giving gesture at 3.5s, which two people share attention? A: Person 0 and Person 2 Q: Across 1.530.0s, who initiates both showing gestures and joins every gaze event? A: Person 0 Table 10: Task examples in our 16-category social-reasoning benchmark. Each row shows one representative MCQ-style questionanswer pair (only the correct answer text is shown for brevity). Difficulty progresses Easy Hard along the rows within each modality (T1T2 Easy, T3T4 Medium, T5T6 Hard; analogous for G/J). A.6 Social Reasoning QA Generation GRASP questionanswer pairs are generated from the unified social graphs using Gemini 3.1 Pro [24]. The goal is to convert structured gaze, gesture, and joint gazegesture events into natural-language social reasoning questions whose answers remain directly verifiable from event metadata. Each QA instance is anchored to one or more source events and includes time range that identifies the relevant visual evidence. We use the 16-category taxonomy defined in 3.2 and Tab. 10, covering gaze reasoning (T1T6), gesture reasoning (G1G6), and joint reasoning (J1J4). QA generation is adaptive to the event density of each graph. Clips with only few gaze or gesture events generate simple perceptionoriented questions, such as gaze target identification, gaze event classification, gesture recognition, and gesture type classification. Clips with richer event timelines generate harder temporal, reciprocal, frequency, sequence, group-attention, and joint reasoning questions. Joint reasoning questions are generated only when the graph contains temporally linked gazegesture pairs. The full QA generation prompt is shown in Fig. 27. All generated QA pairs are validated before inclusion. We discard malformed multiple-choice questions, questions with missing or invalid time ranges, answers containing speculative or affective language, invalid person references, and categorymodality mismatches. The correct answer is derived from structured graph fields such as event type, participant IDs, initiator and target IDs, timestamps, and gazegesture pair links, rather than from free-form generated descriptions. Metadata such as source event IDs, time ranges, and internal reasoning fields is used for verification and filtering. A.7 Quality Control and Human Validation GRASP uses multiple levels of quality control to reduce noise from automatic annotation. First, all gaze and gesture events are filtered by confidence before graph construction. Second, gesture annotation follows conservative protocol that skips ambiguous gestures and avoids inferring intent from non-gesture cues. Third, graph construction removes invalid temporal boundaries, duplicate events, and malformed event records while preserving gazegesture pairs required for joint reasoning. 18 Table 11: Human validation results on GRASP-Bench. Four evaluators audit approximately 25% of the benchmark in category-balanced manner. We report the percentage of QA instances judged visually supported, temporally grounded, and unambiguous. Gaze Reasoning Gesture Reasoning Joint Reasoning Difficulty Validity (%) Cat. Cat. T1 Gaze Target ID Easy T2 Gaze Event Class. Easy T3 Mutual Gaze T4 Gaze Following T5 Temporal Gaze T6 Group Attention Gaze Avg. Medium Medium Hard Hard 64.3 57.1 73.3 88.2 42.9 62.5 65.6 G1 Gesture Recog. G2 Gesture Type G3 Gesture Temporal Medium G4 Reciprocal Gesture Medium G5 Gesture Frequency Hard Hard G6 Sequence Chains Gesture Avg. Difficulty Validity (%) Cat. Easy Easy 73.3 86.7 78.6 93.8 80.0 60.0 78.9 Difficulty Validity (%) Medium J1 Gaze-Gest. Align. Medium J2 Gaze Resp. to Gest. J3 Eye Contact / Inter. Hard J4 Joint Person Dynamics Hard Joint Avg. 86.7 81.2 71.4 80.0 80.0 graph-level health check verifies that required structured fields such as event type, participants, start time, end time, and confidence are present across the corpus. We additionally conduct human validation as an independent reliability audit. We randomly sample approximately 25% of GRASP-Bench in category-balanced manner. Four human evaluators participate in the study. As shown in Fig. 6, evaluators inspect each QA instance with the corresponding video clip, participant IDs, temporal context, category label, question, and answer options. They judge whether the provided answer is visually supported, temporally grounded, and unambiguous. Tab. 11 summarizes the validation results. The audited subset obtains an overall validity of 74.2%, with gesture and joint reasoning showing higher validity rates of 78.9% and 80.0%, respectively. Gaze reasoning is comparatively more challenging, reflecting the difficulty of verifying fine-grained gaze direction, temporal ordering, and group attention in dense multi-person scenes. This validation serves as an independent sanity check on dataset reliability. The final dataset relies on structured event fields, confidence filtering, and deterministic validation rules, with human validation providing an additional estimate of QA quality. Figure 6: Human validation interface. Evaluators inspect each QA instance with the corresponding video clip, participant IDs, temporal context, category label, question, and answer options, then verify whether the QA pair is visually grounded and unambiguous.

sections.title: B Detailed GRASP Data Statistics
sections.content: We summarizes the scale, composition, and construction yield of GRASP. Our video sources span diverse multi-person social settings, including multi-party TV dialogue [43, 81], social intelligence 19 reasoning [88], multi-speaker conversation [60], embodied multi-person behavior3 [57], and social deduction gameplay [39, 7]. We additionally augment the social deduction domain with online video sources, since these videos contain dense multi-person interactions with rich gaze and gesture signals. Figure 7: Dataset-level scale across the six source domains. We report the number of source videos, retained gaze events, detected gestures, and generated QA pairs for each dataset. The y-axis is shown in log scale to make datasets of different sizes comparable. Figure 8: Distribution of retained gaze event types and gesture types. Gaze events are filtered at confidence 0.9, and gestures are filtered at confidence 0.85. Across these sources, GRASP contains 46,158 processed videos. From these videos, we construct structured social representations by extracting person-consistent gaze trajectories and deictic gestures, then integrating them into temporally ordered social graphs anchored to tracked participant identities. The pipeline detects 429,663 raw gaze events, retains 215,378 high-confidence gaze events using confidence threshold of 0.9, and retains 88,234 gesture events using confidence threshold of 0.85. After temporal merging and event deduplication, the pipeline produces 290,148 QA pairs across 16 categories. The final training data contains 238,296 multiple-choice QA pairs for RL training and 51,852 open-ended QA pairs for SFT training. B.1 Per-Dataset Scale Fig. 7 shows the per-dataset scale. In particular, Social Deduction Game contains 4,044 videos but contributes 127,033 gaze events and 30,006 gestures, reflecting the dense multi-person interaction patterns in this domain. 3We use only the multi-person subsets of Embody3D: multi-person conversation, day-in-the-life, and scenarios. 20 Figure 9: QA category distribution. The benchmark contains 16 categories: six gaze categories (T1T6), six gesture categories (G1G6), and four joint reasoning categories (J1J4). Bar colors indicate modality, and markers indicate difficulty. Figure 10: Data construction yield. The pipeline starts from source videos, detects raw gaze and gesture events, filters high-confidence gaze events, merges timeline events, and generates QA pairs and per-QA video clips for training. B.2 Gaze and Gesture Event Distributions As shown in Fig. 8, the retained gaze events cover five event types: gaze following, attention capture, joint attention, mutual gaze, and sudden gaze shift. Gaze following is the most frequent category, accounting for 31.2% of retained gaze events, followed by attention capture at 23.1%. Gesture annotations cover four types: pointing, reaching, showing, and giving. Pointing is the dominant gesture type, accounting for 52.5% of all detected gestures, which is consistent with its role as frequent cue in social and referential interaction. 21 Figure 11: Average gaze and gesture event density per video. Social Deduction Game has the highest event density due to continuous multi-person social interaction. B.3 QA Category Distribution The QA taxonomy is organized into three groups: gaze reasoning, gesture reasoning, and joint reasoning. Gaze categories include target identification, event classification, mutual gaze recognition, gaze following detection, temporal gaze reasoning, and group attention dynamics. Gesture categories cover recognition, type classification, temporal reasoning, reciprocal patterns, frequency, and sequence chains. Joint reasoning categories require relating gaze and gesture events over time, including gaze-gesture temporal alignment, gaze response to gesture, eye contact during interaction, and cross-modal person dynamics. We show their distribution in Fig. B.4 Construction Yield Fig. 10 summarizes the full construction pipeline. The confidence filter retains 215,378 of 429,663 raw gaze detections, corresponding to 50.1% retention. The retained gaze events and detected gestures are then merged into 181,926 timeline events. Since each timeline event can support different question forms, including single-event recognition, temporal comparison, sequence reasoning, and cross-modal reasoning, the final number of QA pairs is larger than the number of merged timeline events. B.5 Event Density and Training Distribution Fig. 11 reports the average number of retained gaze events and detected gestures per video across source domains. Event density varies substantially across domains. Social Deduction Game has the highest average density, with 31.42 gaze events and 7.42 gestures per video. Other datasets are sparser, typically containing fewer than eight total events per video. This variation is expected because the source datasets differ in interaction structure, video duration, and the frequency of visible social cues. Fig. 12 summarizes the distribution of MCQ training examples by modality and difficulty. Easy examples are mostly direct gaze or gesture recognition questions, while medium and hard examples require temporal or cross-modal reasoning. Joint reasoning does not include easy questions by design, since these examples require relating gaze and gesture events or reasoning about person-level interaction dynamics. Overall, the constructed data provides 145,049 gaze QA pairs, 91,536 gesture QA pairs, and 53,504 joint reasoning QA pairs. In terms of format, the final data is primarily multiple-choice, with 238,296 MCQ examples and 51,852 open-ended examples. The MCQ split is used for RL training, while the open-ended split is used for SFT. 22 Figure 12: Distribution of MCQ training examples by modality and difficulty. Open-ended examples are excluded from this heatmap because they are used for SFT and are categorized as hard reasoning examples. Table 12: Comparison of GRASP with existing multimodal social understanding benchmarks. Our dataset scales significantly beyond prior works while distinctly focusing on fine-grained, participantlevel non-verbal grounding. Benchmark Input Grounded Cues #Samples #QAs Core Focus GazeVQA [79] Image Gaze 122K 410K Re-label GazeFollow [68]. Static gaze target underSocial-IQ 2.0 [83] Video - 1.2K 7.5K Unconstrained social situations, explicit human reastanding in single frames soning SocialGesture [7] SIV-Bench [37] Video Gesture - Video 10K 2.7K 10K Multi-person deictic gesture understanding 5.4K Social relation theory, state reasoning, dynamics preSocial Genome [56] Video SocialOmni [86] Video Omni-MMSI [46] Video V-Social [49] R3-VQA [61] MimeQA [44] Video Video Video - - - - - - diction 272 1.5K Grounded multi-step reasoning traces and external knowledge 2.2K Omni-modal conversational interactivity (who, when, how) 5K Re-label MMSI [40]. Identity-attributed interaction 956 and multi-party speaker reference Social relation recognition and grounding in long-form videos 4.8K Mental state estimation and social causal reasoning 806 Social reasoning for silent theatrical storytelling 2K 7K 214 316 221 GRASP (Ours) Video Gaze, Gesture 46K 290K Grounded participant-level events and dynamic social tracking B.6 Comparison with Specialized Social VQA Benchmarks As summarized in Table 12, GRASP occupies unique position in the social AI landscape. Unlike single grounded cues datasets such as GazeVQA [79] and SocialGesture [7], which excel in static spatial gaze target or gesture understanding and localization, GRASP emphasizes the temporal dynamics of social events (e.g., joint attention and turn-taking). Furthermore, while reasoning-centric benchmarks like R3-VQA [61] and Social Genome [56] offer deep cognitive insights, their limited scale often restricts their use to evaluation rather than post-training. GRASP provides the necessary scale (46K videos) and multi-modal grounding (gaze and gesture) to facilitate the alignment of MLLMs with nuanced human social behaviors.

sections.title: C Additional Analyses and Experimental Details
sections.content: C.1 Training Details We train our models with the two-stage procedure described in 4. First, we perform supervised fine-tuning on the open-ended GRASP subset to warm up the model with the structured reasoning format. We then apply GRPO-based reinforcement learning on the multiple-choice training split, 23 using answer correctness, output-format consistency, structural grounding, and social grounding as the reward components. The detailed hyperparameters are summarized in Tab. 13. For both SFT and RL, input videos are sampled at 2 fps and training is performed in bfloat16 with AdamW, cosine learning-rate scheduling, gradient clipping, and DeepSpeed ZeRO-2. The SFT stage is trained for two epochs with learning rate of 2 106, while the RL stage is trained for one epoch with learning rate of 3 106. For GRPO, we use = 8 rollouts per query, KL penalty of 0.05, mean-centered advantage normalization, and advantage clipping to 5.0. The reward weights are set to 1.0/0.1/0.05/0.2 for accuracy, format, structural grounding, and social grounding, respectively. Table 13: Detailed training hyperparameters. Config SFT RL (GRPO) Input frames LR scheduler Warmup steps Optimizer Global batch size Learning rate Weight decay Gradient clipping Training precision DeepSpeed Epochs Max generation length 2 fps Cosine with warm-up 256 2 fps Cosine with warm-up 200 AdamW (β1=0.9, β2=0.999) AdamW 32 2 106 1 103 1.0 bfloat16 ZeRO-2 2 32 3 106 1 103 1.0 bfloat16 ZeRO-2 1 1024 Number of rollouts KL penalty β Advantage normalization Advantage clipping Reward weights 8 0.05 Mean-center 5.0 1.0 / 0.1 / 0.05 / 0.2 C.2 Other Benchmark Details We use MMSI [40], Online-MMSI [45], and TVQA+ [43] to test whether GRASP-Bench transfers beyond in-domain gaze/gesture reasoning to related forms of human-centered video understanding, including person-reference tracking in multi-party conversation and spatio-temporally grounded video QA. MMSI is built from real multi-party social deduction game videos with 151 YouTube games totaling 14.8 hours and 48 Ego4D games totaling 7.3 hours. It defines three dialogue-based tasks: Speaking Target Identification (STI), Pronoun Coreference Resolution (PCR), and Mentioned Player Prediction (MPP). These tasks ask models to identify who is being addressed, referred to, or mentioned, with 4,087 STI, 3,182 PCR, and 3,832 MPP instances across the YouTube and Ego4D subsets. In this game setting, references occur in conversations shaped by accusation, defense, hidden-role claims, persuasion, and shifting group attention. Although gaze and gesture are not the labels being predicted, they are part of the visible evidence humans use to track who speaker is addressing or discussing [TODO: CITE]. Online-MMSI uses the same source data and tasks but restricts the input to context before the target utterance, giving us related test of person-reference tracking in setup more realistic to active dialogue. TVQA+ comes from TVQA [42], which contains 152K QA pairs from 21.8K clips across 460 hours of six TV shows. TVQA+ augments the Big Bang Theory subset with spatial grounding annotations, yielding 29,383 multiple-choice questions from 4,198 clips, 148,468 annotated images, 310,826 bounding boxes, and 2,527 visual concept categories. The benchmark asks models to answer questions from subtitles and video while localizing relevant moments and grounding visual concepts such as people and objects. The questions are not strictly social reasoning, but they are human-centered, asking who did something, what someone was holding or wearing, where someone was, what direction someone turned, or what object was involved. These questions often require interpreting peoples actions and relations to objects, space, and time, where gaze, gesture, body orientation, and movement can provide useful visual evidence. 24 Table 14: Participant-ID corruption test on GRASP-Bench. C.3 Participant-ID Corruption Analysis potential concern is that SGR could be exploited by simply repeating participant IDs that already appear in the question, rather than grounding the reasoning trace in the video. To diagnose this shortcut, we conduct participant-ID corruption test on GRASP-Bench. For each MCQ example, we consistently remap all Person IDs in the question, answer options, and metadata answer text, while leaving the video unchanged and preserving the correct answer letter. This keeps the textual format and answer-position prior intact, but breaks the alignment between textual Person IDs and the visual Person IDs overlaid in the video. If model mainly relied on text-only ID shortcuts, its accuracy should remain stable under this corruption. In contrast, visually grounded model should degrade when the answer requires mapping textual participant references to the correct people in the video. As shown in Tab. 14, both SGR-trained models drop substantially under participant-ID corruption. Qwen3-VL-8B+SGR drops from 50.4% to 32.6%, and Qwen3.5-9B+SGR drops from 52.6% to 32.0%. The degradation is strongest in categories that require resolving specific participants, such as gaze following (T4), temporal gaze reasoning (T5), gesture recognition (G1), reciprocal gestures (G4), and joint person dynamics (J4). For example, T4 drops by 22.4pp and 29.9pp, G4 drops by 27.2pp and 35.5pp, and J4 drops by 29.3pp and 11.4pp for the two models, respectively. The category-level pattern also clarifies when corruption should or should not affect performance. Categories with weaker direct dependence on participant identity, such as mutual gaze duration or some group-attention questions, show smaller or mixed changes. In contrast, categories that require tracking who looks at whom, who gestures to whom, or who participates in both gaze and gesture events consistently show large drops. This diagnostic does not rule out every possible form of reward gaming, but it directly addresses the ID-repetition shortcut: the final SGR-trained policies depend on the consistency between textual participant references and visually grounded participant identities, rather than merely reusing Person IDs from the question. C.4 Reasoning Length Breakdown To understand how accuracy and reasoning length relate, we plot the accuracy/length in Fig. 13 and perform Pearson correlation analysis. We find no correlation (Pearson = + 0.039) between accuracy and average reasoning length for all baseline models for the collection of social reasoning tasks in 25 Figure 13: Accuracy compared against average reasoning length for all baselines. GRASP-Bench. This runs counter to findings for other VQA tasks where longer reasoning helps performance. Here longer reasoning is not necessarily better reasoning. Meanwhile, our method simultaneously shortens the reasoning length for the our models and improves the accuracy. C.5 SGR Error Profile Figure 14: Error profile for SGR compared against difficulty of the questions for the base models. To understand the difficulty of the GRASP-Bench tasks, we group cases into buckets defined by how many base models were correct, and the accuracy of the our models per bucket was computed as shown in Fig. 14. This gives an estimation of how hard the GRASP questions are for our comparison models off the shelf and allows us to evaluate the SGR error profile. There are three primary takeaways: The SGR performance tracks along the scale of difficulty described by the base model performance. This suggests there are other things contributing to the complexity of the benchmark tasks which are not being captured by our reward. There exist hard cases that all other models fail on where we recover using the SGR approach (like Fig. 1), but there are also easier cases where all other models are correct but our approach fails (examples in Appendix. D.3). 26 Our models separately follow similar error profiles but our two models are making different errors, creating an opening for future work to investigate the characteristics of the errors being made by each model when supported by SGR.

sections.title: D Qualitative Comparison
sections.content: We provide qualitative examples to complement the main results in the paper. The examples illustrate how GRASP-Bench questions are grounded in fine-grained gaze and gesture events, how different models reason over the same multi-person interaction, and where our method still fails. D.1 GRASP-Bench Examples We present representative GRASP-Bench examples in Fig. 23, 24, and 25. These examples cover gaze reasoning (T1T6), gesture reasoning (G1G6), and joint gazegesture reasoning (J1J4), respectively. Each example shows the video context with tracked participant IDs, the relevant non-verbal cue, and the corresponding questionanswer pair. Together, they illustrate the range of GRASP-Bench, from direct perception questions such as gaze target or gesture type recognition to harder questions requiring temporal ordering, reciprocal interaction, and gazegesture alignment. D.2 Reasoning Trace Comparison with Baselines We compare reasoning traces from our SGR-trained models against representative supervised, reasoning, and RL post-training baselines. Fig. 15, 16, and 17 show GRASP-Bench examples across easy, medium, and hard difficulty levels. In addition to GRASP-Bench, we include examples from MMSI [40] and TVQA+ [43]. Fig. 18, 19, and 20 show the three MMSI tasks: speaker target identification (STI), pronoun coreference resolution (PCR), and mentioned-player prediction (MPP). Fig. 21 shows TVQA+ example. Across these cases, baselines often rely on salient participants or dialogue priors, while our models more consistently ground their responses in the relevant participants and visual/social cues. D.3 Failure Cases Fig. 22 shows representative failure cases of our method. In the first example, the gesture is visually ambiguous: the queried interval contains point-like phase of reaching motion, while later frames make the reaching/recoil pattern clearer. Such cases are challenging because the distinction between pointing and reaching can be subtle even under human inspection. In the second example, the target interval contains multiple gaze events from the same participant, with several people involved in nearby social activity. The model misses one relevant gaze cue and therefore grounds its answer in incomplete evidence. These examples suggest that remaining errors often arise from ambiguous gesture semantics and limited resolution of dense multi-person gaze dynamics.

sections.title: E Broader Impacts
sections.content: GRASP and SGR are intended to advance research on multimodal social understanding. By encouraging models to reason about who interacts with whom from gaze and deictic gestures, our work may benefit human-centered video understanding, assistive agents, collaborative robotics, and socially aware interfaces where participant-level grounding is important. At the same time, social reasoning from video requires careful interpretation. GRASP is constructed from publicly available or research-oriented video sources, and people are represented through anonymous participant IDs rather than real identities. The benchmark focuses on event-level visual reasoning, such as who looks at whom or who gestures toward whom, and is not designed for identifying individuals, profiling people, or inferring private mental states. Since gaze and gesture can be ambiguous, culturally variable, and sensitive to occlusion or camera viewpoint, models trained or evaluated on GRASP should not be used for high-stakes decisions about people. Our dataset and models may also inherit biases from the source videos and from the foundation models used in the construction pipeline. Social settings, camera styles, gestures, and interaction norms in the data may not represent all populations or contexts. In addition, automatic gaze and 27 gesture extraction can introduce annotation noise in complex scenes. We mitigate these issues through confidence filtering, structured validation, and subset human feedback, but improving diversity and event reliability remains important future work. Finally, GRASP uses multimodal and language models for gesture annotation, event description, and QA generation. To reduce unsupported inferences, ground-truth answers are derived from structured fields such as participant IDs, event types, timestamps, and gazegesture links, rather than free-form generated text. We believe that releasing GRASP as documented research benchmark can help the community study socially grounded MLLMs more transparently, while making the limitations and intended scope of the dataset explicit. 28 Figure 15: Qualitative comparison on an easy GRASP-Bench example.
